findings.description: buffer = self._record_buffer aliases the live list; del self._record_buffer[:] then empties it before rendering, so export_text(clear=True) returns an empty string and permanently destroys the recorded segments — take a copy before clearing.
The missing-formatter branch now formats {alias!r}, but alias is not defined in get_formatter_by_name; unknown aliases raise NameError instead of the documented ClassNotFound that callers handle.

The renderable assignment and its paired refresh are no longer atomic with respect to _RefreshThread.run, which takes the same lock before calling refresh(); a concurrent update can interleave so the display shows stale or torn state, and the GIL-atomicity claim does not hold for non-CPython runtimes.

Measurement.get no longer clamps the renderable's __rich_measure__ result with with_maximum(max_width) or normalize(), so measurements can exceed the console width or violate minimum <= maximum; layout consumers such as table column sizing assume the clamped invariant and now miscompute widths.

get_lexer_by_name now caches the lexer INSTANCE keyed only by alias, so the first caller's options (stripnl, encoding, filters — see Lexer in pygments/lexer.py) silently apply to all later callers, and one stateful instance is shared; the class, not the instance, is what can be cached.

update_completed is computed from the advance argument alone; when a caller updates progress via completed= (as _Reader and download flows do) the delta is 0.0, no ProgressSample is appended, and Task.speed/ETA silently stop working for that path.

update_computed is computed from advance only, ignoring the completed parameter. When update() is called with completed=..., the speed-estimation ProgressSample records 0 or the wrong delta, corrupting progress speed tracking.

findings.description: Removed .with_maximum(_max_width) capping so Measurement.get() can return a measurement whose maximum exceeds options.max_width. Renderables like Text (which ignores max_width in __rich_measure__) now leak widths wider than the available console width, causing downstream layout algorithms to allocate more space than exists.

Removing the lock from update() enables a race condition where concurrent update() and refresh() calls can interleave, causing refresh() to read an outdated self._renderable value while update() is setting a new value. This violates the invariant that update() with refresh=True should atomically set the new renderable and have it displayed.

findings.description: update_completed is computed before all mutations to task.completed, so when both advance and completed are provided (with completed having a lower value), the progress sample records an incorrect delta instead of the actual delta. The original code computed the delta after mutations to ensure correctness, defeating the performance optimization.

The change removes the .normalize() call that ensures measurement.minimum <= measurement.maximum, violating the semantic invariant that a valid measurement's minimum width should never exceed its maximum width. This causes table.measurements to return invalid measurements (e.g., minimum=46, maximum=20) when width constraints are active, breaking tests that expect normalized measurements.

update_completed is computed before mutations and only considers advance, ignoring completed parameter. When completed is set without advance (e.g., completed=100.0, advance=None), update_completed is 0.0 instead of the actual delta, causing incorrect speed estimation.

The error message references `alias!r` but the parameter is named `_alias`. This causes a NameError crash when an unknown formatter alias is provided, instead of raising the documented ClassNotFound exception.

Measurement.get() no longer normalizes or clamps the result from __rich_measure__, violating the previous guarantee that minimum <= maximum and maximum <= options.max_width. Custom renderables returning invalid measurements will propagate unnormalized values to callers that may rely on these invariants.

Progress.update() now sets update_completed = advance (line 1442) and therefore ignores direct updates via the 'completed' parameter. As a result, when callers set completed=<new_value> (without advance), no progress sample is recorded for the change and speed estimates become incorrect; previously update_completed was computed as task.completed - completed_start and correctly captured completed-parameter changes (prior code computed the delta after mutations and appended it at line 1465).

findings.description: Measurement.get now returns renderable.__rich_measure__() output raw (assigned at line 107) without calling normalize(), so the returned Measurement may violate invariants (minimum <= maximum and minimum >= 0), breaking callers' assumptions.

Measurement.get no longer clamps the measurement to options.max_width (previously with_maximum(_max_width)); returning render_width directly at line 110 can yield maximum > options.max_width, violating layout bounds and leading to incorrect width calculations.

update_completed only considers the advance parameter, so calling update() with completed (or with both advance and completed) records wrong or missing progress samples, breaking speed estimation. When completed is set without advance, no sample is appended; when both are set, the sample uses advance instead of the actual delta.

Measurement.get no longer normalizes the __rich_measure__ result, allowing minimum > maximum to propagate. This breaks the invariant that minimum <= maximum and causes tests/test_table.py::test_render_table to fail because a Table with width=20 returns Measurement(46, 20) instead of Measurement(20, 20).

Measurement.get no longer clamps the __rich_measure__ result to options.max_width, so renderables that ignore max_width (e.g., Text) leak widths exceeding the console. For example, Measurement.get(console, options, Text('hello world')) returns maximum=11 when options.max_width=10, breaking the invariant that measurements fit the available space.

The refresh() method still holds self._lock around self.renderable access, but renderable() is now property-locked around self._lock. Without self._lock covering the update path, a race can occur where _refresh_thread reads an incompletely constructed renderable, leading to an AttributeAccessError if the referenced object is partially initialized or deleted.

The update_completed delta is computed before all mutations that could change task.completed, but advance and completed are applied after assignment, allowing update_completed to ignore completed mutations.

The original code guaranteed minimum >= 0 and minimum <= maximum by calling normalize() after retrieving the measurement from __rich_measure__; removing this invariant check allows invalid measurements to propagate, and drops the with_maximum(_max_width) call which was intended to constrain widths to options.max_width.

The original unpacking creates distinct tuple temporaries before returning, ensuring the tuple is fully evaluated. Using direct indexing (pad[0], pad[1], pad[2], pad[3]) on the length-4 branch returns a new tuple from the original reference without a temporary object, changing the observable behavior of repeated calls (different bytecode structure, memory layout, and potential effects on inlining/optimization).

The change replaces the delta computation task.completed - completed_start with a fixed update_completed value that is only 0.0 when advance is None. This silently drops all delta calculations when advance is omitted, causing ProgressSample to have no duration data, which may lead to incorrect speed estimation or silent failures.
Undefined variable 'alias' when ClassNotFound is raised, causing a NameError crash.

The change omits the normalization step that ensures minimum <= maximum, breaking the invariant that Measurement objects always satisfy minimum <= maximum. Code relying on this invariant (maximum < 1 check) may produce misleading width estimates.

buffer is assigned as a reference to self._record_buffer, not a copy. When del self._record_buffer[:] clears the list, buffer becomes empty too, causing export_text to always return an empty string regardless of recorded content. The intent to 'take ownership' requires buffer = list(self._record_buffer).

Line 2185 assigns buffer = self._record_buffer, which copies only the reference, not the contents. When clear=True, line 2187 clears self._record_buffer[:] inside the lock, making buffer (which references the same list) empty. Subsequent iteration over buffer after the lock releases produces empty text, losing all recorded data.

The _plugin_lexer_cache stores instantiated lexer objects keyed only by alias, so a subsequent call with the same alias but different **options returns the cached instance created with the original options, silently ignoring the new caller-provided options.

The plugin lexer cache stores a lexer instance created with specific **options, but returns this cached instance on subsequent calls regardless of the **options passed. This violates the function contract that the lexer is instantiated with the caller-provided options, causing options from the first call to silently apply to all subsequent calls with the same alias.

findings.description: export_text takes a reference to self._record_buffer (line 2185) then clears that same list in-place under the lock (lines 2186-2187). Because buffer aliases the internal list, clearing empties buffer before rendering outside the lock, causing exported text to be empty when clear=True. The code should copy the buffer contents instead of aliasing to avoid data loss.

get_lexer_by_name caches and returns a single lexer instance per alias (see _plugin_lexer_cache at line 131 and assignment at line 136) regardless of the **options** passed. Previously, plugin lexers were instantiated on each call with the provided options; the new behavior can return an instance created with different options, causing incorrect behavior.

get_lexer_by_name now caches instantiated plugin lexer objects and returns the same instance for an alias (see cache lookup at line 131 and storing at line 136). This breaks the previous invariant that callers receive a newly instantiated lexer configured with the provided options — later calls with different options will incorrectly get the previously cached instance.

When clear=True, deleting elements from the shared record buffer after capturing buffer reference breaks the invariant that export_text renders exactly the current recorded content. Subsequent calls may see inconsistent, truncated, or duplicate segments because they consume a snapshot while another thread mutates the original buffer.

If get_lexer_by_name raises ClassNotFound on a valid alias (e.g., due to options that fail during instantiation), subsequent calls will return a partially initialized instance from the cache without re-checking instantiation, masking the failure.

The cached instance may have been created with outdated options if subsequent calls use different options, breaking the invariant that the cache returns the same object structure for repeated identical alias lookups.

findings.description: buffer = self._record_buffer creates an alias, not a copy; del self._record_buffer[:] on line 2187 clears the same list in-place, so buffer is always empty when clear=True, making export_text return an empty string.

Plugin lexer cache stores instances keyed only by alias, ignoring **options. A second call with the same alias but different options returns the first cached instance instead of constructing a new one with the requested options, violating the documented contract that 'The lexer is given the options at its instantiation.'

_plugin_lexer_cache caches and returns the same lexer instance regardless of **options, so subsequent calls with different options silently get an instance configured for the first call's options, violating the function contract.
